coredata.dc:title: 3D-printed polyamide-H2TiO3 composite for selective lithium adsorption: Insights from experimental and theoretical dynamic simulations studies

coredata.dc:description: Global decarbonization has increased the demand for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), leading to a high demand for lithium a critical raw material in the EU. Efficient lithium recovery, especially from water sources, is essential due to the growing demand need for LIB recycling. Adsorption technology is effective for this purpose, but traditional powder adsorbents present operational challenges. To overcome this, 3D printing allows for the preparation of solid monolith adsorbents with improved mechanical stability and reusability. In this work, a polyamide-lithium titanium oxide composite (PA2200-Li2TiO3) was 3D-printed using selective laser sintering technology. The composite was characterized using SEM-EDS, TEM, XRD, Raman spectroscopy, TGA and XPS. The adsorbent exhibited up to 87 % lithium adsorption efficiency at a concentration of 20 mg/L and showed high selectivity for lithium over sodium (Li/Na ≈ 37.4) and potassium (Li/K ≈ 60.5). A mechanism involving Li+ ion adsorption and transport through Li2TiO3’s structural channels has been proposed, with dynamic simulations suggesting that lithium ions preferentially diffuse through these channels due to their geometric and energetic configuration. Thermodynamic studies confirmed an endothermic adsorption process with enhanced performance at higher temperatures. Regeneration tests demonstrated only a 7 % reduction in adsorption efficiency after three cycles, indicating structural stability and the potential for scalable application in lithium recovery processes.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Global decarbonization has increased the demand for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), leading to a high demand for lithium a critical raw material in the EU. Efficient lithium recovery, especially from water sources, is essential due to the growing demand need for LIB recycling. Adsorption technology is effective for this purpose, but traditional powder adsorbents present operational challenges. To overcome this, 3D printing allows for the preparation of solid monolith adsorbents with improved mechanical stability and reusability. In this work, a polyamide-lithium titanium oxide composite (PA2200-Li2TiO3) was 3D-printed using selective laser sintering technology. The composite was characterized using SEM-EDS, TEM, XRD, Raman spectroscopy, TGA and XPS. The adsorbent exhibited up to 87 % lithium adsorption efficiency at a concentration of 20 mg/L and showed high selectivity for lithium over sodium (Li/Na ≈ 37.4) and potassium (Li/K ≈ 60.5). A mechanism involving Li+ ion adsorption and transport through Li2TiO3’s structural channels has been proposed, with dynamic simulations suggesting that lithium ions preferentially diffuse through these channels due to their geometric and energetic configuration. Thermodynamic studies confirmed an endothermic adsorption process with enhanced performance at higher temperatures. Regeneration tests demonstrated only a 7 % reduction in adsorption efficiency after three cycles, indicating structural stability and the potential for scalable application in lithium recovery processes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium was designated as a critical raw material by the European Union (EU) in 2020, and as of the assessment committee’s latest report in 2023 (European Commission, 2023), it continues to hold this status. Lithium can be extracted from various sources, such as salt and petroleum brines, as well as minerals. Additionally, other sources of lithium such as clays, geothermal water and lithium containing secondary sources (Meshram et al., 2014), can be utilized. These secondary sources encompass wastewater from battery production and battery recycling facilities, hydraulic fracturing fluids and petroleum brines (Safari et al., 2020).

Lithium is utilized in industry with the largest share in lithium-ion batteries (other applications are glass and ceramics, grease and lubricant, metallurgy, air conditioning, medicine, and polymers) (Meshram et al., 2014). Over the past decade, the lithium-ion battery (LIB) sector has accelerated in growth, driven by the decarbonization goals outlined in the 2016 Paris Agreement, resulting in an increased demand for raw materials (Windisch-Kern et al., 2022). In 2021 the demand for lithium (in lithium carbonate equivalent) was estimated at 465 000 metric tons, a figure projected to reach 2.1 million metric tons by 2030 (Statista, 2021). In addition to rapidly growing lithium market, the EU has set a minimum collection rate of 70 % for LIBs to be achieved by each member state by the end of 2030 (European Parliament, 2021). This highlights the urgency for advancements in lithium recycling technologies.

Technologies for lithium recovery from liquid sources include solar evaporation, ion exchange and solvent extraction, as well as recovery of lithium ions by adsorption (Wang et al., 2022). In comparison to other methods, adsorption offers the advantage of requiring no pretreatment of the feed solution, achieving high recovery rates from low concentration solutions, and the possibility to concentrate the raffinate by adjusting the eluent/feed ratio (Safari et al., 2020). Current studies in lithium adsorption are focused on the recovery of lithium ions from liquid sources using lithium-ion sieves (LIS), such as lithium-manganese oxides (LMO) (Gao et al., 2023; Li et al., 2021), lithium-titanium oxides (LTO) (Chen et al., 2017; Sun et al., 2023), and layered double hydroxides (LiAl-LDH) (Chen et al., 2021a, Chen et al., 2021b; Paranthaman et al., 2017). According to studies, HMO exhibits a higher adsorption capacity and adsorption–desorption rates than HTO but have less structural stability. Pretreatment of LMO to obtain lithium-ion sieve results in dissolution of Mn due to its multiple valences. LTO has less loss of Ti, due to stronger Ti-O bonds, better resistance to alkali/acid media and better recyclability (Zhang et al., 2021).

Application of LISs in a form of powder is one of the challenges in adsorption due to the loss of material during the process (Hong et al., 2013). For this reason, there have been attempts to embed powder LISs into foams, membranes, nanofibers, spheres, and pellets using binder materials such as polyvinylchloride, polysulfone, polyurethane, polyacrylonitrile, polyvinylidene fluoride, silica, agar, and chitosan (Xiao et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2021). However, binder materials tend to cover the active sites of the adsorbent which decreases the adsorption performance (Zhang et al., 2021). In addition, methods of embedding, such as electrospinning, granulation and foaming involve use of strong organic solvents for dissolution of polymers, such as dimethylacetamide (Zhang et al, 2021), dimethylformamide (Wei et al., 2020), tetrahydrofuran (Lawagon et al., 2019), n-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (Lin et al., 2019), that creates additional problem of toxic waste management and utilization.

Additive manufacturing is one of the technologies that allows the embedding of powder adsorbents in the form of monolithic structures. 3D printing technology has been recently applied to adsorption (Li et al., 2019; Mohd Yusoff et al., 2022; Pereira et al., 2022). In comparison to powders, stable monolithic structures are easier to operate in both batch and column adsorption modes. The design of the adsorbent plays important role. Complex porous structures provide high surface area and better availability of active sites for adsorption, that improves the mass and heat transfer (Kim et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2019).

In selective laser sintering (SLS) a 3D object is built layer-by-layer from a powder building material. The model of a future 3D object is designed via CAD program and mathematically sliced into layers, which turns the model into G-code that is read by a printer software (Han et al. 2022). The building material is heated up in a heating bed and laser beam is applied to selectively fuse layers of powder with each other. The energy absorbed from the laser results in coalescence of the particles of the powder material and lead to formation of a solid structure (Partee et al., 2006). For adsorption purposes, SLS technique is particularly advantageous since it offers a wide range of operating parameters. The optimized printing conditions make powder particles sinter partially, creating interparticle space, that results in microporous structure. Also, additives to the powder matrix are getting attached on the surface of the printed object, that allows to avoid the problem with covered active adsorption sites (Lahtinen et al., 2019).

Lahtinen et al. conducted series of studies involving SLS printing technique for liquid adsorption. In their work, they successfully 3D-printed filters with use of pure polyamide (nylon-12) for recovery of gold from acid leachate of electronic waste (Lahtinen et al., 2017). In another work, the adsorbents from mixture of polypropylene and ion exchange resin were printed for recovery of precious metals (Au, Pt, Pd, Sn) from electronic waste as well (Lahtinen et al., 2018). In both studies, 3D-printed printers show good recovery rates and chemical stability. Ibebunjo et al., 2024 used SLS printing for preparation of adsorbent for recovery of copper from copper tailings. They functionalized recycled polymer with chelating resin that work as selective adsorbent for copper.

In relation to recovery of Li with 3D printing, not so much of research is published. One of the recent studies is by Yu et al., (2023) in which they created a 3D-printed ceramic-type scaffold via direct ink writing method (DIW), using slurry containing Li2TiO3, SiO2 and mineral binders. The method was based on printing of slurry with direct ink writing method with following calcination of the scaffold at different temperatures. In the current study, an efficient 3D-printed polyamide-H2TiO3 (3D-PA@HTO) composite was successfully prepared using SLS technology. The resultant 3D-PA@HTO was characterized using SEM-EDS, XRD, Raman spectroscopy, TGA and XPS to assess its structural, stability and morphological properties. Subsequently, the adsorption efficiency of the optimized 3D-PA@HTO was evaluated through a series of experiments to determine its effectiveness in removing Li+ ions, particularly in the presence of competing sodium and potassium ions. Finally, a potential mechanism involving adsorption of Li+, Na+, and K+ ions in 3D-PA@HTO was proposed using dynamic simulation.

Lithium titanate Li2TiO3 powder was supplied by Sigma-Aldrich, USA. Commercial polyamide powder PA2200 was provided by EOS GmbH, Germany. Lithium hydroxide monohydrate (≥99.0 %) and Lithium chloride anhydrous (≥98.0 %) were purchased from Fluka Chemie GmbH, Switzerland. Metal salts NaCl (99.9 %) and KCl (99.9 %) were supplied by VWR, Inc., Belgium. Stock solutions of 1000 mg/L containing only Li+ and mixture of Li+, K+, and Na+ were prepared in deionized water. Working solutions of different concentrations were prepared by dilution of stock solutions. pH values were adjusted with use of 1 M NH4OH and 1 M HCl.

3D-printed structures were designed using SOLIDWORKS and loaded into slicing software Ultimaker Cura 4.10 to generate G-code for the printing process. 3D-printed monolith adsorbents were prepared using powder bed fusion process through the application of SLS with a professional 3D printer (Sharebot SnowWhite2, Italy) equipped with a 14 W CO2 laser. The supporting matrix was one of the common polymers for SLS printing polyamide PA2200, also referred to as nylon 12. Commercial PA2200 is a fine powder with an average particle size of 50 μm characterized by higher crystallinity and a higher melting point compared to typical polyamides (Table 1 ). Active component was inexpensive, non-toxic and commercially available powder Li2TiO3.

Preliminary to printing, pure PA2200 was subjected to an aging process. Aging of powder involves a cycle of heating and cooling to stabilize the physical characteristics of the powder. Subsequently, the aged PA2200 powder was thoroughly mixed with Li2TiO3 (LTO) powder, to obtain dry mix with 10 % content of LTO. The ratio of active material to binder was selected based on published studies. Layer thickness for printing was 0.3 mm. The laser power was 33 % with a power rate of 55 000 (2200 mm s−1) and border rate of 64 000 (2600 mm s−1). The temperature of printing was 157–159 °C.

The resulting 3D prints were cleaned from the unsintered powder dust with pressurized air to obtain a 3D-printed PA2200-Li2TiO3 composite, further referred as 3D-PA@LTO (Fig. 1 ). Pellets of the adsorbent were prepared to compare adsorption performance. This was done by extruding the working powder into filament, which was then shredded into pellets. The percentage of Li2TiO3 in the mixture of powders is 10 % in both the 3D prints and the pellets.

Surface morphology and elemental analysis of 3D-printed samples was done with scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi SU3500, Tokyo, Japan) equipped with energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) operating at a voltage of 10–15 kV, a vacuum pressure of 100 Pa, and a variable magnification. In prior to SEM-EDS analysis samples were covered with Au for conductivity. Transmission electron microscopy (Hitachi HT7700, Hitachi High-Technologies Corporation, Tokyo, Japan) was used to characterize the microstructure of the samples. X-ray powder diffractometer with Co Kα radiation. Raman spectroscopy (Kaiser Raman RXN1, Kaiser Optical Systems, Inc). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out with a linear temperature rise at a constant flow rate 40 mL/min helium atmosphere with heating rate of 10 °C/min from 25 °C to 900 °C (TGA-DTA, STA 449C/4/MFC/G/Jupiter®, NETZSCH-Gerätebau GmbH, Selb, Germany). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was used to investigate composition of the materials before and after adsorption. The spectra were measured using PHI Quaterna II scanning XPS microscope (ULVAC-PHI, Physical Electronics, Japan) equipped with an Al Kα (1486.6 eV) source. The 3D-printed samples were sliced onto pieces ∼1 mm in height and mounted onto a holder using screws. The survey spectra of the materials were measured within binding energies range 0–1100 eV with pass energy 224 eV, a speed of 50 ms per step, and a resolution of 0.5 eV per step. The high-resolution Ti2p line was recorded in BE range of 454–468 eV with pass energy of electrons 20 eV. The analysis of the spectra was done using CasaXPS 2.3.15 (Casa software Ltd., Devon, UK).

Prior to adsorption experiments, prepared composites 3D-PA@LTO were treated with 0.25 M HCl for elution of Li+ to obtain 3D-printed PA2200-H2TiO3 lithium-ion sieves (further referred as 3D-PA@HTO). During elution the ratio of adsorbent to liquid was 100 mg/20 ml (10 mg of active component/20 ml). Adsorbents were put to acid and shaken at room temperature for 24 h.

Adsorption studies include effects of pH, adsorption isotherm and kinetics, effect of temperature, as well as recyclability and selectivity of the adsorbent in the presence of competitive alkali ions. Concentrations of metal ions before and after adsorption were determined by AAS spectroscopy (3300 AAS, Thermo Fischer Scientific, USA). The adsorption and desorption performances were estimated by adsorption capacity (qe), adsorption efficiency (E), distribution coefficient (Kd), and separation factor (
α
M e
L i
).

pH effect on lithium adsorption was studied at pH values from 7.0 to 13.0 using HCl (1 M) and NH4OH (1 M) for adjustment. S/L ratio for adsorption was 20 mg of active adsorbent / 10 ml solution. 3D-PA@HTO ion sieves were put to solution with Li+ concentration of 20 mg/L and shaken for 24 h at room temperature.

Isotherm of adsorption was obtained in the range of Li+ concentrations from 5 to 100 mg/L. 20 mg of active material were shaken in 10 ml of lithium solution at pH 12 for 24 h at room temperature. Residual concentration of Li ion after adsorption was measured, and adsorption capacity was calculated according to Eq. (1).

Kinetics of adsorption process was studied at pH 12, with Li concentration of 20 mg/L in 500 ml batch with S/L ratio of 20 mg of active material per 10 ml solution. The kinetic curve was obtained by collecting samples at certain time intervals.

Selectivity of adsorbents was determined by conducting adsorption in synthetic solution containing alkali ions (Li+, Na+, K+) at pH 12. The adsorbents were shaken at room temperature for 24 h with S/L ratio of 20 mg/10 ml. Selectivity was calculated with use of separation factors of metal ions in a multicomponent system according to Eqs. (7), (8) (Lin et al., 2019).

SEM images of 3D-printed samples were collected and presented in Fig. 2 . Particles of Li2TiO3 are visible on the images of 3D-PA@LTO sample and were examined at different magnification. Fig. 2(a–f) shows the surface of the 3D-printed samples from the top. The designed microstructure is observed with particles of PA sintered with each other as expected after 3D printing. The particles of Li2TiO3 are observed on the surface of PA particles. Some agglomeration is visible. Images (c) and (d) show the surface of 3D-PA@HTO ion sieve after Li ion elution with 0.25 M HCl. The structural damage seen after acid treatment in 3D-PA@HTO (a crack in the particle) is related to PA particle. Meanwhile, H2TiO3 particles obtained after the acid leaching of Li from Li2TiO3 as part of 3D-PA@LTO themselves did not exhibit any noticeable structural degradation.

Images (e) and (f) show the surface of 3D-PA@LTO after adsorption of lithium ions from 20 mg/L solution at pH 12. Significantly lower amount of LTO is observed on the surface of PA after adsorption. Elemental mapping of 3D-PA@LTO sample is presented in Fig. 3 . Presence of C and N is related to polyamide, while Ti and O are in the content of Li2TiO3. The high amount of gold is explained by using it as a cover to improve the conductivity of the samples.

Fig. 4 shows TEM images of 3D-PA@LTO (a, b) and 3D-PA@HTO (c, d) at different magnification. The adsorbents have a microscopic morphology of aggregated spherical particles with particle sizes in the range of approximately 200–250 nm. The calculated lattice d-spacing values for 3D-PA@LTO and 3D-PA@HTO were 0.480 nm (Fig. 4b) and 0.570 nm (Fig. 4d), respectively, which correspond to the (002) planes of Li2TiO3 (Qin et al., 2024) and (002) planes of H2TiO3 (Ren et al., 2025).

XRD spectra of 3D-PA@LTO and 3D-PA@HTO composite before and after adsorption of lithium, as well as the spectra of pure LTO are presented in Fig. 5 . XRD analysis revealed clear differences between the patterns of LTO and the 3D-printed PA@LTO composite.

In pristine Li2TiO3, Bragg reflections corresponding to planes (002), (1̅33), (2̅04), (2̅06) and (062) confirm the monoclinic structure with a space group of C2/c. The (002) corresponds to the ordering of Li-Ti-O layers in β-phase Li2TiO3 (Mukai et al., 2023). The (1̅33) lattice plane characterizes the Li-Ti-O framework ordering of lithium and titanium ions in the LiTi2 slab of the monoclinic structure (Lakshmi-Narayana et al., 2019). The characteristic LTO reflections, particularly the (1̅33) and (2̅06) planes, are clearly present in the 3D-PA@LTO composite; however, they appear at slightly lower 2θ angles and exhibit significantly reduced intensities.

LTO peaks did not reappear after adsorption process, which means that 3D-PA@HTO did not undergo structural changes after adsorption process (Marthi et al., 2021). The peak between 20° and 30° is corresponded to polyamide. The doublet transformation into singlet is observed. This can be explained by the SLS printing process, that affects the hydrogen bonds between molecular chains of polyamide, that reduces crystallinity (Yang et al., 2021).

Raman spectroscopy was used to see the changes in breaking and formation of chemical bonds in 3D-printed samples. Raman spectra of LTO (a), 3D-PA@LTO before adsorption (b), 3D-PA@HTO (c), 3D-PA@HTO after adsorption of Li in 20 mg/L solution (d) and 3D-PA@HTO after adsorption in Li+, Na+, K+ solution (e) are presented in Fig. 6 at the range of 100–800 cm−1 (a) and 100–3425 cm−1 (b), respectively. The bands in the region up to 250–550 cm−1 are responsible for Li-O and O-Li-O stretching and banding vibrations and are clearly visible in pure Li2TiO3 and 3D-PA@LTO. The bands between 550 and 700 cm−1 are related to stretching and banding vibrations of Ti-O (Xu et al., 2014). Li-O/O-Li-O bands disappeared after elution of lithium, when doublet band at 404, 420 cm−1 transformed into singlet. Transformation of Ti-O bond into doublet after delithination is observed and was noted in literature (Marthi et al., 2022). Li-O reappears in very weak intensity after adsorption of Li in pure Li solution and alkali metal system (Li+, Na+, K+), that can support the fact that ion exchange takes place. Peaks at 1066 cm−1 correspond with C–C-O stretching vibrations, at 1111 cm−1 with C–C skeletal stretching vibration, at the ranges of 1290–1440 cm−1 and 2700–2900 cm−1 CH2 groups, and 1640 cm−1 and 3300 cm−1 for N–H stretching vibration, that all belong to polyamide.

The survey spectra of the materials are represented in Fig. 7 . Number of characteristic lines were found in XPS spectra of the pristine samples including Si2p and Si2s, P2p and P2s, C1s, N1s, Ti2p, O1s. Lines related to the presence of the P in the sample disappeared after washing with HCl. Insignificant amounts of P and Si are related to antioxidants and flow agents in the content of PA2200 (Shackleford et al., 2021). Simultaneously, Ti2p peak became more pronounced. Due to the low concentration of the Ti on the materials surface and low cross-section coefficient of Li1s line, no specific peak related to the Li-ions in the sample were found in survey spectra of the samples. The Ti2p high-resolution line (Fig. 7b) was recorder for materials before and after alkali metal adsorption. The position and symmetry of the Ti2p line suggest presence of Ti4+ in the materials. Broadening of the peaks after alkali metals adsorption confirmed adsorption of the Na-ions.

TGA analysis was conducted to determine the thermal stability of the samples (Fig. 8 ). TGA of samples show that LTO is stable within the given range of temperatures, while 3D-printed PA composite showed the best stability at 450 °C. Pure PA powder, as expected, showed less thermal stability with degradation starting at 350 °C. In 1st derivative of the TGA thermograms (Fig. 8b), the peak of the3D-printed sample is the highest, while pure PA is the least thermostable. TGA test also showed the real mass of LTO in 3D-PA@LTO sample, which was 10.93 %, as expected, based on thepreparation method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on Li+ adsorption
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 9 represents the effect of pH on Li+ adsorption. The selected pH of 7–13 is explained by the studies on lithium adsorption with H2TiO3, where significant changes in adsorption performance were observed only within the given range (Wang et al., 2009). Maximum adsorption efficiency was achieved at pH 12. Lithium adsorption mechanism can be explained in terms of ion exchange. According to thereaction Eq. (9), a stoichiometric amount of H+ was released into the solution during lithium adsorption (Lin et al., 2019). At lower pH released H+ accumulates in the solution hindering further release of H+ from the HTO ion sieve and reducing Li+ uptake. In the solution with higher alkalinity (pH > 9), OH– ions in the solution are bounded with H+, that results in their neutralization, favoring ion exchange and Li+ adsorption. This explains the higher adsorption efficiency of lithium at higher pH as was observed in other studies (Wei et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2021). However, when pH exceeds 12, lithium adsorption slightly decreased. It can be explained by very high OH– concentration in the solution and as a result increased ionic strength of the solution, that causes accumulation of OH– ions onto adsorbent surface and hindered ion exchange (Du et al., 2023; Xie et al., 2022; Yao et al., 2024). To further investigate this, we conducted a follow-up experiment at fixed pH 13 while varying the ionic strength by adjusting NaCl concentration from 0 to 1.0 M (Fig. S1). The results showed a consistent decline in lithium uptake with increasing ionic strength, confirming that the adsorption reduction at high pH is influenced by changes in the solution environment. This observation aligns with previous reports on lithium-selective adsorbents under similar condition (Chen et al., 2021a, Chen et al., 2021b; Du et al., 2023; Yao et al., 2024; Zhang et al., 2023).
(9) H2TiO3 + 2Li+ → Li2TiO3 + 2H+

Kinetics of adsorption was conducted with theinitial concentration of Li+ 20 mg/L in 500 mL batch volume. Adsorption kinetics represents the adsorbed amount of Li+ as a function of contact time. Experimental adsorption kinetics data was fitted with the PFO and PSO model (Fig. 10 a). The parameters of adsorption kinetics are listed in Table 2 . The time profile shows fast adsorption during thefirst two hours followed by a gradual levelling and adsorption equilibrium reached within 12 h. Fast adsorption in the beginning of the process is explained by abundancy of available adsorption sites that favours a rapid H+/Li+ ion exchange. With increase in concentration of adsorbed Li+, concentration of Li+ in the solution decreases that slows down the adsorption rate and leads to stabilization of the process at equilibrium state. The maximum adsorption capacity of 3D-printed PA@HTO reached 0.881 mg/g. Comparing the two models, PSO fits the data more effectively based on the calculated regression coefficients R2. The calculated equilibrium adsorption capacity (0.898 mg/g) was similar to the experimental value (0.881 mg/g). This result suggests that adsorption on 3D-printed PA@HTO composite is controlled mainly by ion-exchange process between H+ and Li+ ions. The result aligns well with previous studies of lithium adsorption with use of both pristine HTO (Qin et al., 2024) and HTO-based composites (Chen et al., 2021a, Chen et al., 2021b; Marthi and Smith, 2021).

Adsorption isotherms express the relationship between the adsorbed ions on the surface of the adsorbent and the concentration of the adsorbate in the solution. The adsorption capacity of 3D-printed PA@HTO increased with the increasing of the initial concentration of Li ions. Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models were used to fit the experimental data (Fig. 10b). The adsorption isotherm parameters are summarized in Table 3 . Based on the regression coefficient R2 values, the isotherm models showed adequate fit to the obtained data with more accuracy by Freundlich model (R2 = 0.988) compared to Langmuir model (R2 = 0.891). The better fitting of Freundlich model indicates that adsorption tends to occur in the multilayer manner on the heterogeneous surface of the adsorbent. Many studies observed adsorption on HTO-based adsorbents that aligns better with the Langmuir model (Hao et al., 2023; Lawagon et al., 2016; Qin et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2024). However, in some works (Marthi and Smith, 2021; Wei et al., 2020) Freundlich fitting worked better for the modelling. The Freundlich model describes multilayer adsorption on heterogeneous surface of the adsorbent (Bonilla-Petriciolet et al., 2017), which aligns with the structural characteristics of 3D-PA@HTO. The heterogeneity of the HTO particles is evident from SEM images and the particle size distribution, which show a wide variation in particle sizes and morphologies. These structural irregularities contribute to a range of adsorption site energies, facilitating multilayer adsorption behavior. In comparison to 3D-printed samples, adsorbent in the form of pellets at the same conditions resulted in much lower removal efficiency with qm of 0.5 mg/g, while for 3D prints the value was 1.480 mg/g, based on the Freundlich model.
Despite the low value of adsorption capacity, adsorption efficiency was high with anaverage lithium uptake of 87 % at a concentration of 20 mg/L.

Adsorption efficiency of Li ions on 3D-PA@HTO composite was investigated at different temperatures 298.15 K (25 °C), 308.15 K (35 °C), 318.15 K (45 °C) and 328.15 K (55 °C). Eqs. (10) and (11) were used to determine the free Gibbs energy, enthalpy, and entropy of adsorption (Piccin et al., 2017):

where K is the adsorption equilibrium constant, T (K) and R are the temperature and gas constant (8.314 J/mol K), respectively. Fig. 5 represents removal efficiency of Li+ as a function of temperature (a) and lnK as a function of 1/T (b). The thermal effect reveals that an increase in temperature enhances the adsorption performance (Fig. 11 a). The dependency of adsorption performance from temperature shows gradual increase in adsorption efficiency with increase in temperature as follows: 83,28 % at 298.15 K, 86.54 % at 308.15 K, 90.55 % at 318.15 K, and 90,77 % at 328.15 K, respectively.

Thermodynamic parameters are listed in Table 4 . The negative values of ΔGo energy decrease as the temperature increases, which indicates that Li+ adsorption on 3D-PA@HTO is a spontaneous process with increasing driving force (Fig. 11b). The positive ΔHo suggests that adsorption process is endothermic. In aqueous solutions, Li ions exist in a hydrated form and the destruction of the hydrated shell causes theabsorption of heat (Wei et al., 2020). The positive ΔSo indicates an increase in the degree of molecule disorder in the solution, which is favorable for the adsorption process.

To determine the selectivity of adsorbents, a solution containing equimolar concentrations of each metal ion (Li+, K+, Na+) was used at pH of 11.37 for 24 h (Fig. 12 a). The selective adsorption of Li+ on 3D-PA@HTO was conducted in a lithium solution containing competitive Na+ and K+, which are typically present in industrial lithium-containing solutions and brines. The results are presented in Table 5 . The equilibrium distribution coefficient (K d) for Li+ was extremely higher than that for competing ions (49.48 for Na+, 30.61 for K+). The separation factors (
α
M e
L i
) of the competing ions relative to Li ion were very high. The high adsorption selectivity of Li+ on the 3D-PA@HTO composite can be attributed to the ion-sieving effect of the HTO lattice. Overall, these results indicate that 3D-PA@HTO could be used for the selective adsorption of Li ions from streams containing Na and K.

Recyclability of the 3D-printed lithium-ion sieves was evaluated through adsorption–desorption experiments repeated for three cycles (Fig. 12b). The adsorption performance decline after the third cycle was 7 %. Meanwhile desorption efficiency increased. This indicates that the studied materials are durable and resistant to process conditions and do not lose their metal binding properties.

Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations offer a powerful computational tool for examining the dynamic behaviour of materials at the atomic level. By solving Newton's laws of motion, MD tracks the time evolution of interacting atoms, allowing the study of processes such as ion diffusion. This method helps calculate properties like diffusion coefficients and mean displacements, providing valuable insights into material performance for applications like battery technology (Sun et al., 2020; Tan et al., 2024), polymer nanocomposite (Takari et al., 2021) and environmental pollution (Largo et al., 2020).

MD combines classical mechanics and core–shell potential models to simulate systems with thousands of atoms under controlled conditions (temperature and pressure), using force fields that represent atomic interactions. These include bonded forces (covalent, angular, and torsional) and non-bonded (van der Waals and electrostatic interactions), which are defined by force fields such as UFF (Rappé et al., 1992), COMPASS (Sun et al., 1998), Amber (Love et al., 2023), and ReaxFF (van Duin et al., 2001).

Lithium titanate (Li2TiO3) belongs to monoclinic crystal system, with a space group of C2/c. Its lattice parameters are approximately a = 5.062 Å, b = 8.7876 Å, c = 9.7533 Å, and β = 100.212°, with slight variations depending on temperature and synthesis conditions. In the crystal structure, lithium ions (Li+) occupy 4e Wyckoff positions, often sharing these sites with titanium ions (Ti4+), leading to partial cation disorder (Yu et al., 2015). Titanium ions are positioned in octahedral sites, also occupying 4e Wyckoff positions, where they are coordinated by oxygen atoms in a TiO6 octahedral arrangement. The oxygen ions are located in the 8f Wyckoff positions, bridging the lithium and titanium sites and forming a distorted octahedral position (see Fig. 13 ). In this study, we conducted a detailed computational analysis of the lattice structures of X2TiO3 (X = Li, Na, and K), focusing on the site occupation by Li+, Na+, and K+ ions. A 2x2x1 supercell was employed for the lattice to provide a more comprehensive representation of the structure and ion interactions. The comparison of the resulting structures was based on key geometric and energetic parameters. The construction of the X2TiO3 lattices involved a critical geometric optimization step performed using the COMPASS (Elkabous et al., 2024; Sun, 1998) contained in the Forcite module of the Biovia Materials Studio V.7.0 software package (Biovia, 2017)(Elkabous et al., 2023). The Ewald summation method was applied to accurately capture electrostatic interactions, while atom-based options were used for Van der Waals forces. After the geometric optimization, dynamic simulations were carried out on the optimized 3D structures using the same force field and parameters. These simulations were conducted within the NPT (constant number of particles, pressure, and temperature) ensemble to replicate realistic thermodynamic conditions, allowing the system to equilibrate at constant temperature and pressure. The dynamic simulations enabled the study of ion motion and trajectories within the lattice, providing crucial insights into the diffusion behavior and stability of Li+, Na+, and K+ ions within the X2TiO3 materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Geometric optimization of X2TiO3 (X = Li, Na, and K)
ce:section.ce:para: The optimized lattice parameters of Li2TiO3, Na2TiO3, and K2TiO3 reveal clear differences, primarily due to the varying ionic radii of the Li+, Na+, and K+ ions. After geometric optimization, the lattice parameters for Li2TiO3 were determined to be a = 10.2167 Å, b = 10.2167 Å, and c = 9.7646 Å, with a density of 3.151 g/cm3. For Na2TiO3, the lattice parameters remained similar in the a and b directions but slightly expanded along the c axis, resulting in a = 10.2167 Å, b = 10.2167 Å, and c = 9.8346 Å, with a higher density of 3.787 g/cm3. K2TiO3 exhibited the largest lattice parameters, with a = 11.4390 Å, b = 11.4390 Å, and c = 11.0112 Å, and a corresponding density of 3.815 g/cm3. These values are summarized in Table 6 and the equilibrium geometries of the three materials are illustrated in Fig. 14 .

This systematic increase in lattice parameters from Li2TiO3 to K2TiO3 can be attributed to the ionic radii of the constituent alkali metal ions. Specifically, as the ionic size increases, the larger cations apply more spatial demand on the TiO6 octahedra, leading to an expansion of the lattice structure. This effect is particularly significant in the c-axis, which increases progressively across the three materials, as larger cations require more space to accommodate their size.

Furthermore, density variations also align with this trend, where Na2TiO3 has a higher density than Li2TiO3 due to the larger Na+ ions and their higher atomic mass. Although K2TiO3 has the largest lattice parameters, it also possesses the highest density, suggesting that the increased volume is offset by the mass of the larger K+ ions.

The radial distribution functions (RDFs) (Terban and Billinge, 2022) for K+, Li+, and Na+ ions during their exchange with hydrogen (H+) in the H2TiO3 structure clarify important factors influencing the selectivity and diffusion characteristics of these ions. The RDF represents the probability of finding an ion at a given distance from a reference point, typically an oxygen atom in this case, as it reflects the local structure surrounding the ions. In our study, we analyzed the RDF profiles of the exchanged ions within the H2TiO3 matrix to explore their local environment, mobility, and interaction with the lattice.

The RDF profiles of K+, Li+, and Na+ ions, visualized in Fig. 16 . The RDF for Li+ ions highlights a sharp peak at around 2.0 Å, with a maximum RDF value of approximately 5.3. This prominent and narrow peak reflects the strong interaction between Li+ and the surrounding oxygen atoms. The small ionic radius of Li+ allows it to be tightly bound to the TiO3 structure, resulting in a well-defined coordination sphere. The sharpness of the peak indicates highly localized bonding, implying that Li+ ions are stabilized within the H2TiO3 matrix, enhancing their selectivity during ion exchange. However, the tightly bound nature of Li+ suggests limited mobility, leading to slower diffusion rates. The subsequent peaks, though less intense, further confirm a structured environment around the Li+ ions, suggesting that they do not diffuse easily, but maintain a strong electrostatic interaction within the lattice. Alternatively, the RDF of Na+ ions display a broader primary peak at 2. , with a maximum value of about 5.0. This peak, though still sharp, is less defined than the Li+ RDF, indicating a less flexible, moderately coordinated environment. Na+, with its intermediate ionic size, forms stable interactions with the H2TiO3 lattice and does not bind as strongly as Li+. The broader peak suggests that Na+ ions have a more relaxed coordination structure, offering a balance between selectivity and mobility. The RDF profile demonstrates that Na+ ions diffuse less readily than Li+ due to weaker electrostatic interactions, but still retain enough stability to make them effective in selective ion exchange processes. Meanwhile, the RDF profile for K+ ions is notably different, with a much broader and less defined peak at 2.8 Å, and a maximum value of about 4.8. This broad peak reflects the weaker interaction between K+ ions and the surrounding oxygen atoms. The larger ionic radius of K+ means it cannot bind as strongly or as closely as either Li+ or Na+, resulting in a diffuse and unstructured coordination environment. This diffuse bonding suggests that K+ ions exhibit higher mobility within the lattice, making them less selective in ion-exchange processes but allowing for faster diffusion. The broad and low-intensity peaks disclose that K+ ions are more loosely associated with the TiO3 structure, facilitating quicker ion movement but at the expense of binding stability.

Selectivity and recyclability are key parameters that determine the potential industrial application of adsorbent. Currently, the applied technologies of lithium recovery from industrial leachates are solvent extraction and ion exchange. Adsorption has potential as a passive separation method that overcomes solvent extraction and ion exchange by more environmentally friendly and less energy consuming method (Weng et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2016).

The developed material was compared to other reported materials, which are presented in Table 8 . The main difference was that in contrast to other materials, the composite in this work has only 10 % of theactive component, while in other studies active material content was up to 75 %. This can explain relatively low adsorption capacity in comparison to reported composites. The advantages of the material are an easy preparation method, with theopportunity to adjust the dimensions of adsorbents and preparation of big amounts, no need for pretreatment and postprocessing of materials, no need to work with toxic solvents prior to printing, unlike in granulation, electrospinning, and membrane preparation methods, where organic solvents are used. High selectivity of the HTO active component can be beneficial in therecovery of Li from multicomponent solutions. However, low adsorption performance in comparison to powder adsorbents, makes it more reasonable to be applied in low-concentrated brines and seawater, rather than industrial wastewater with high Li content. Low-capacity adsorbents can be used for the recovery of lithium from unconventional resources such as petroleum brines, geothermal water, and hydraulic fluids (Safari et al., 2020; Seip et al., 2021).

The results obtained in this work were compared to the selectivity adsorption of some 3D-printed adsorbents from the literature (Table 9 ), selected for their composition, which is similar to that of the studied 3D-PA@HTO. It was found that the lithium selectivity of 3D-PA@HTO is among the highest reported. Therefore, considering its selectivity performance, low cost, chemical and mechanical stability, and ease of regeneration, it can be concluded that 3D-printed PA@HTO, produced using SLS technology, proves to be a promising and cost-effective adsorbent for the selective separation of lithium ions from aqueous solutions.

An efficient and cost-effective method for fabricating high-performance 3D-printed PA2200-Li2TiO3 composites has been developed using selective laser sintering (SLS) technology. The conclusions drawn from SEM images were corroborated by XRD, Raman, and XPS analyses, collectively confirming the successful synthesis of 3D-PA@LTO monolithic adsorbents. These 3D-PA@LTO monoliths exhibited exceptional chemical and mechanical stability, as well as a promising Li+ ion exchange capacity. Moreover, they demonstrated excellent selectivity for lithium over sodium (Li/Na ≈ 37.4) and potassium (Li/K ≈ 60.5). Dynamic simulations, along with experimental validation, revealed a precise mechanism by which Li+ ions preferentially diffuse through the structural channels of H2TiO3, driven by the material's unique geometric and energetic configuration. This mechanistic understanding strengthens the foundation for advancing the application of these monoliths in efficient lithium recovery. Additionally, this approach offers several practical advantages, including a simple fabrication process, the use of relatively inexpensive raw materials, and a robust monolithic structure that facilitates ease of use in batch adsorption systems. These benefits, combined with the material's stable performance, strongly support the potential for further exploration of 3D-PA@LTO composites in large-scale lithium separation and recovery technologies.
